data.title: Pentair: Peaking Pricing Power Could Be A Problem
data.description:
I would consider Pentair plc (PNR) a quality company in general, but there are some issues here that need to improve if the shares are going to break out of a fairly nondescript long-term performance pattern. Management has done well on margins, but free cash flow has only kept up with revenue growth, volume hasn’t really grown at all since 2021, and the company has lagged its industrial peers on organic growth every quarter since 2022.

I was neutral on Pentair with my last article and thought it was worth considering on a pullback. Investors indeed got that pullback and would be up more than 25% from those lows. However, the shares have risen only about 15% overall, lagging an industrial sector that has really perked up lately on expectations of stronger short-cycle recoveries.

Pentair isn’t really short-cycle-sensitive, and I’m worried that the company will have to pull back some on pricing in 2026 after years of relatively aggressive moves. I’m also concerned that important industrial markets like agriculture, food service, and mining aren’t really going to help too much. With the shares around my fair value estimate, I can see some appeal as other industrials get more expensive, but I’d like to see better organic growth to complement what management has done with margin improvements.

Weak Volumes But Some Margin Improvement In Q4

On balance, this wasn’t a particularly exciting quarter for Pentair, as the company’s organic growth once again lags the broader sector and margin improvement is becoming more of an expectation than a real sentiment driver.

Revenue rose a little less than 4% this quarter, good enough for a small beat versus Street expectations, but once again weaker than the broader industrial space (which is seeing around 4.5% growth on average).

Gross margin improved by 160bp to 40.4% as the company continued to use price to offset tariff pressures, and this drove the earnings upside for the quarter. Adjusted operating income rose 9%, with margin up 90bp to 24.7%, as SG&A spending was a little higher than expected. Segment-level profits rose almost 9% with a similar 90bp margin improvement to 27.1%.

The Pool business saw 9% core revenue growth, virtually all of which came from price. Segment profits improved 11%, with margin down 20bp to 33.6%.

Water Solutions revenue declined 4% on a 9% volume contraction, with Commercial down 4% on a core basis and Residential down 1%. Segment profit was flat, with margin up 130bp to 23.9%.

Flow revenue rose 4%, with 4% growth in Residential, 12% growth in Commercial, and 12% growth in Industrial. Segment-level profit rose 22%, with margin up 240bp to 22.8%.

Sluggish Guidance And Some Cloudy End-Market Outlooks

Pentair definitely disappointed the Street in a quarter where beat-and-raise guidance has been more the norm. Management’s revenue guidance midpoint was about 1% below the Street, and investors were disappointed in the outlook for flat volumes and more caution on pricing (up 3% to 4%, largely offsetting inflation and tariffs). With EPS guidance also about 1% below the Street at the midpoint, it would seem that margin leverage has slowed as a driver of outperformance.

Looking at 2026, I don’t see a lot of markets that stand out as clear positive drivers.

Pentair has done well in Pool on a consistent basis, but even acknowledging efforts in areas like product development (including convenience-enabling automation, AI, and IoT), I’m concerned that the company has been perhaps too aggressive on pricing. The “strained consumer thesis” is pretty well accepted at this point, and the housing market remains relatively soft, which doesn’t bode well for volumes.

An improving outlook for non-resi construction should drive some momentum in areas like fire suppression, but the restaurant capex environment is still poor, and that’s going to weigh on an important end-market.

Industrial markets are more mixed.

While Pentair’s products fit well with an ongoing drive toward automation in mining, overall mining capex spending is looking a little iffy at this point, though commodity prices are at a level that should encourage volume/capacity growth.

Food and beverage should be an area of relative strength. Membrane and filtration technology offers leverage to the current trend toward higher-protein offerings, as protein sources like whey are filtered from milk processing waste streams, but this is a small and emerging business for Pentair on a relative basis. Agriculture is still not looking all that healthy, as weak crop prices disincentivize hardware upgrades (like irrigation) and increased use of productivity chemicals.

The Outlook

My biggest complaint about Pentair may simply be that the positives are well known and appear to be flattening out. Management’s 80/20 continuous improvement initiative has helped lift EBITDA margin from below 20% to over 26% in just three years, but I expect the pace of improvement to slow in the coming years. Likewise, a mid-teens ROIC is comparatively pretty good, but further improvement appears to be slowing, and free cash flow generation has really only been keeping pace with revenue over the last five years (and lagging over the last 10 years).

What this suggests to me is that management may need to be more active in areas like R&D and product vitality. Minimal volume growth since 2021 is a concern, and if margin leverage is starting to peter out, organic growth is going to become a more significant driver. In some ways this reminds me of what got 3M (MMM) into trouble—the company built an extremely strong margin machine but dropped the ball on innovation and saw volumes erode over time as a result.

I’m at the lower end of management’s guidance for 2026 with 3% revenue growth, with some upside if housing and food service markets recover more in the year. I’m expecting around 4% growth over the next three years, and Pentair isn’t really all that well-leveraged to the short-cycle recovery theme. Longer term, I expected annualized revenue of around 4%.

I do expect further EBITDA margin improvement, but at a slowing pace, with around 30% margin in five years and around three points of operating margin leverage over the next three years. With improvements in working capital efficiency, this should drive free cash flow margins into the low-to-mid-20%s, driving around 8% of long-term FCF growth.

Between discounted cash flow and margin/return/growth-driven EV/EBITDA (a forward multiple of 16x), I think Pentair shares are more or less fairly valued with near-term undervaluation of around 5% and long-term annualized total return potential of around 7%-8%.

The Bottom Line

With limited leverage to short-cycle recoveries and a comparatively weak track record where volume and organic growth are concerned, I’m not sure what would drive a strong rerating of Pentair shares other than perhaps some bargain-shopping. Improving residential and commercial markets could help, and the company could deploy more capital into M&A, but as is, this looks like a pretty archetypical “it’s okay, I guess” investment story.

data.title: Wasatch Small Cap Value Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Overview

Small cap value stocks delivered a solid gain in the fourth quarter and finished the year with a double-digit total return. Investors appeared encouraged by falling interest rates and an improving economic outlook for 2026, propelling the Russell 2000 Value® Index—the Fund’s benchmark—to a series of all-time highs through mid-December. Despite the positive showing for the broader asset class, the Wasatch Small Cap Value Fund—Investor Class (WMCVX) declined -3.88% and underperformed the benchmark, which gained 3.26%. The primary reason for the shortfall was our tilt toward higher-quality companies, together with our avoidance of the types of momentum stocks that led the rally.

Our emphasis on quality was the most notable driver of the Fund’s underperformance over the full year, as well. The investment backdrop was characterized by persistent strength in stocks on the lower end of the quality spectrum, as gauged by metrics such as profitability, return on equity and free cash flow, among others. Companies without current earnings performed particularly well during the year. In this sense, our underperformance for the quarter and for all of 2025 was largely a function of the stocks the Fund didn’t own rather than those it held. The phenomenon was perhaps most pronounced in the health-care sector, where highly speculative biotechnology stocks—stocks to which the Fund had no exposure—led the way at the expense of the quality companies held by the Fund. Although our emphasis on quality has weighed on relative performance for several quarters, it has been a key factor in the Fund’s long-term outperformance. We therefore remain true to our patient and long-term approach, rather than attempting to chase transitory market trends.

Details of the quarter

Weakness among holdings in the consumer-discretionary sector weighed on Fund performance in the fourth quarter. For-profit education provider Grand Canyon Education, Inc. (LOPE) was a key detractor, as were Ollie’s Bargain Outlet Holdings, Inc. (OLLI), and Frontdoor, Inc. (FTDR). These stocks, while representing a broad range of businesses, have a common theme: all had performed very well through the first nine months of the year but sold off following earnings reports that were insufficient to fuel continued momentum. Believing the fundamentals of these companies remain strong, we maintain long-term positions.

The health-care sector was also a source of weakness, with Certara, Inc. (CERT)—a supplier to biotechnology development firms—weighing on results for the second consecutive quarter. Investors have been focused on the short-term headwinds caused by the weak funding environment for biotechnology firms, but we remain confident in the company’s longer-term fundamentals and market position. The Fund’s positioning in financials was a further headwind, due in part to a limited weighting in the types of small banks that outperformed, as well as weakness in Shift4 Payments, Inc. (FOUR). The stock came under pressure from worries about potential softness in the consumer sector, obscuring what we believe is more favorable longer-term outlook.

On the positive side, the double-digit return of our group of stocks in the information-technology sector partially offset underperformance in other sectors. Fabrinet (FN), an outsourced provider of optical equipment used in datacenters, gained ground and built on its robust year-to-date return. The company, which is seen as a beneficiary of the artificial intelligence (AI) trend, reported record revenues and issued upbeat forward guidance.

Similarly, Camtek Ltd. (CAMT) exhibited improving fundamentals due to its exposure to the AI infrastructure build-out. CTS Corp. (CTS), a producer of sensors, connectivity components and actuators that are used in broad set of end markets, was another top performer. The stock had been under pressure for much of the year because of its sensitivity to the weak commercial vehicle end market, but we maintained the position on the view that it was very inexpensive relative to its underlying quality and growth. CTS shares rebounded in the fourth quarter, aiding the Fund’s performance.

Outside of technology, Vita Coco Co., Inc. (COCO)—a global leader in coconut water—was the top contributor to Fund performance behind better-than-expected earnings and increased forward guidance. Vita Coco has a supply- chain advantage thanks to management’s focus on establishing long-term contracts with suppliers, which we think can help provide a foundation for a multiyear growth trajectory. (Current and future holdings are subject to risk and change.)

Outlook

We believe the recent market environment, while challenging, has provided the opportunity to position the portfolio in out-of-favor companies that we believe are poised for an upward re-rating in valuations. Our emphasis throughout the year has been on adding new Fallen Angels, our term for growth companies that have hit a bump in the road and whose shares have dropped into value territory. We’ve seen the Fallen Angel opportunity set open and close on a rolling basis over time and have positioned the Fund accordingly. In 2022, for instance, we were very active in deploying capital into Fallen Angels. While this approach initially weighed on results, it ultimately drove strong outperformance in 2023–24. It’s impossible to predict when market conditions will shift, of course, but we believe the allocation to Fallen Angels provides the foundation for improved results in the coming year.

As always, we remain focused on finding opportunities on a company-by-company basis. Rather than trying to assess the likely impact of external factors, we will continue striving to do what we do best: identifying undervalued, high-quality businesses. Our analysis shows that although the Fund has underperformed, the fundamentals of our portfolio holdings have remained firm. We believe this potentially helps set the stage for a period of outperformance once the popularity of speculative momentum stocks subsides.

Thank you for the opportunity to manage your assets.

Sincerely, Austin Bone

data.title: Weyerhaeuser: Too Much Optimism About Recovery Potential
data.description:
Shares of Weyerhaeuser (WY) have been a poor performer over the past year, losing about 10% of their value. The timberlands owner has struggled with weak demand for lumber products given falling construction levels. That said, shares have rebounded sharply from their lows given hopes of a housing recovery as mortgage rates have come down. I last covered Weyerhaeuser in October, rating the stock a “Sell.” While shares initially fell 7% towards my $20 target, they have since reversed and are now 20% higher. With updated financials, now is a good time to see if the bottom is in or if this rally will fade.

In the company’s fourth quarter, Weyerhaeuser lost $0.09 per share, which was $0.04 less than feared, as revenue declined by 20% to $1.5 billion. While a bit better than consensus, this was a weak quarter in absolute terms. EBITDA was down by more than half from last year at $140 million, also down about a third sequentially. As you can see below, results have been weakening as the prolonged nature of the housing downturn limits earnings power and lumber demand. I do not expect a sharp near-term recovery. During the quarter, it divested two properties for $406 million and has an agreement to sell 108,000 acres for $193 million.

Timberlands EBITDA declined by $34 million sequentially to $114 million, and results in the West region were particularly weak, down $30 million to just $45 million. Weaker throughput pressured margins as revenue fell $49 million while expenses fell just $19 million, causing EBITDA margins to compress 5% to 23%. We saw a new low in export revenue, though China did resume some purchases as trade tensions have eased. Still, given elevated trade tensions compared to a year ago, I expect export revenue to remain structurally lower and likely to remain in the ~$70 million range.

Wood products swung to a $20 million EBITDA loss in the quarter. Lumber lost $57 million, OSB (oriented strand board) lost $10 million, and EWP (engineered wood products) earned $49 million. All were sequentially worse. Lumber prices fell 3%, while OSB prices were down 6%, though input costs were also slightly lower. As you can see below, OSB prices have been especially weak, and prices are down by nearly half since mid-2024. This speaks to the magnitude of the demand problem emanating from the housing sector. It is seeing some strength in OSB pricing more recently as prolonged weakness is finally leading to curtailment and reduced supply. Curtailments may help put a floor under pricing.

While OSB and Lumber are purely commodity products, its EWP products have more value-added features, making their profitability a bit more resilient. Realizations are substantially higher than in OSB and Lumber, and while prices have been declining, the magnitude of the drop is less than in OSB. We have seen volumes trend a bit lower as well, given weaker demand. Even as margins are structurally higher here than in OSB/Lumber, EWP is exposed to the same underlying demand trend.

Housing is the key consumer of lumber and wood products, and housing activity has weakened. This has been a simple but powerful driver of weaker results for Weyerhaeuser. As you can see below, the number of homes under construction has been steadily falling, thereby reducing lumber demand. Even after their multiyear decline, construction levels remain above pre-COVID levels, which is why I am unconvinced a bottom is in. KB Home (KBH) noted that builders are reducing spec activity to stabilize supply, and markets like Florida and Texas are still struggling with supply from the post-COVID buildout. Because of this, I do not expect home construction activity to bottom until late 2026 at the earliest, which will delay any turnaround in WY’s earnings.

Finally, Strategic Land Solutions (formerly known as Real Estate, Energy, and Natural Resources) generated $95 million, up 4% sequentially. Results were buoyed by a higher average selling price, which was unusually high, as you can see below. This was because it sold only higher-value properties in its South region. Mix shift can cause this metric to bounce around a lot, and I expect revision in 2026.

In 2025, the company generated $397 million of funds available for distribution, though this was weighed down by a $200 million pension payment. Still, this did not fully cover its $606 million in dividend costs last year. Shares offer a 3.1% dividend yield, and it did $160 million of buybacks in 2025, though just $10 million in Q4.

Because of its weak cash flow, Weyerhaeuser’s balance sheet continues to erode. Net debt is about $5.1 billion, and net leverage is up to 5x from 4.3x last quarter. It targets a 3.5x mid-cycle leverage ratio, so leverage is high, though management would argue that EBITDA is well below mid-cycle levels. Still, we need to see ~40% EBITDA growth just to return leverage to target.

WY is running with excess leverage, expecting a rebound in the business. While excess leverage can be accepted for a period of time during economic weakness, the longer this goes on, the more scrutiny its balance sheet will get. It is also worth remembering that as bad as this environment seems, housing activity is still higher than pre-COVID levels. It is not entirely clear demand is that much worse than “mid-cycle,” and rather, there may be persistent supply gluts due to overbuilding of capacity post-COVID. This would make its balance sheet position more problematic.

Looking forward, management expects similar results in Timberland and just modest growth in Wood Products, given seasonally higher volumes. There should be a $90 million benefit in SLS due to the timing of real estate sales, and quarterly results can move significantly due to timing. 2026 cap-ex will be about $725 million, due to a $300 million EWP project and ~$425 million of run-rate spending. Given this growth spending, I expect net debt levels to rise further this year.

Beyond just this year, Weyerhaeuser has laid out a multiyear target of $3 billion in EBITDA by 2030. Frankly, I am very cautious on the growth plans here. While management said it achieved all of its 2019-2024 5-year plan, the stock today is below pre-COVID levels (though it does pay a dividend). 2025 EBITDA was just $1 billion, so we need to see a 50% improvement just to get back to 2024 levels. Given structural affordability challenges, I struggle to see a path where housing activity booms enough for a tripling of EBITDA over 5 years. We are unlikely to return even to 2024 levels before 2027. The onus is on management to prove it can even approach these targets.

Back in October, I saw the company generating about $550 million of free cash flow. Excluding its EWP expansion project, I still view this as the likely outcome in 2026, given very limited room for volume growth in this housing market. With the EWP construction project, free cash flow is unlikely to cover the dividend, with WY relying on asset sales and its over-leveraged balance sheet to support the payout. Frankly, after the rally in shares, I think WY has gotten ahead of itself, and the persistently sluggish housing environment is likely to be a source of ongoing disappointment. With weak 2026 free cash flow, just a 3.2% dividend yield, and excess debt, I view WY as unattractive, and I recommend using the rally to exit WY with a return to the low $20s likely in my view.

data.title: Thrivent Mid Cap Growth Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Executive Summary

  • Thrivent Mid Cap Growth Fund underperformed the Russell Mid Cap Growth Index during the quarter due underperformance in the Industrials and Financial Services sectors.
  • The Fund had positive performance relative to the benchmark in five of the eleven sectors.
  • For the trailing twelve-month period, the Fund underperformed the Russell Mid Cap Growth Index due to negative stock selection.

Performance factors

Thrivent Mid Cap Growth Fund underperformed the Russell Mid Cap Growth Index during the quarter due to underperformance in the Industrials and Financial Services sectors, partially offset by positive security selection primarily in the Information Technology and Healthcare sectors.

Within the Information Technology sector, the Fund's positive security selection came primarily from our positions in Coherent Corp (COHR) and JFrog Ltd (FROG), as well as Amphenol Corp (APH). All three companies had strong 3Q earnings results and we continue to hold positions in these shares.

In the Healthcare Sector, performance was driven by positions in Natera (NTRA) and Penumbra (PEN). Natera continued to build on its market leading position in cancer MRD (Minimal Residual Disease) while Penumbra further advanced its innovative patented CAVT (Computer Assisted Vacuum Thrombectomy) technology into peripheral VTE (Venous Thromboembolism). Both companies are addressing large market opportunities with differentiated offerings and are taking market share.

Communications Services, Consumer Staples, Energy, Healthcare and Information Technology sectors were the five positive contributors in the quarter, while among the remaining underperforming sectors, Industrials and Financials were most notable. The largest negative impact within Industrials was an un-owned cash burning aerospace company. In Financials, the largest detractor was our position in Robinhood Markets (HOOD) which sold off in November on lower-than-expected crypto currency related revenue. We remain positive on the long-term opportunity for Robinhood and note that the position was among our top positive contributors for the full year 2025.

Portfolio outlook

While the Fund did underperform for the full year 2025, we are encouraged by the outperformance vs. the Russell Mid Cap Growth Index during the 2H 2025 and have continued efforts to reposition the Fund. Since the end of 2Q 2025, we have exited 26 and initiated 21 new positions.

The Fund remains focused on identifying quality mid cap growth stocks with large total addressable markets, positive product or market cycles, and differentiated business models.

  • U.S. fixed income markets finished the year on a strong note, registering solid returns across various sectors. The bullish narrative surrounding markets centered on additional easing from the U.S. Federal Reserve (Fed) and a resilient macroeconomic and consumer backdrop. The Fed delivered an additional 50 basis points (BP) of rate cuts during the quarter, including a 25 bp December cut that occurred despite market odds falling sharply late in November. Real GDP grew at a +4.3% annualized rate in the third quarter, supported by strong consumer spending. Other tailwinds included better-than-expected corporate earnings and revision trends, sustained hyperscaler investment supporting the AI theme, and an improvement in trade dynamics. However, several headwinds, including signs of labor market softening and consumer affordability pressures, housing weakness, data uncertainty from the government shutdown, and relatively stretched investor sentiment, partially offset these positive themes. 1
  • Over the quarter, short-term investment grade corporate 2 credit spreads widened by 4 basis points (bps), short-term commercial mortgage-backed securities (CMBS) 3 spreads widened by 1 bp, and short-term asset backed securities (ABS) 4 spreads widened by 4 bps. The 2-Year U.S. Treasury yield decreased from 3.61% to 3.48% and the 10-Year U.S. Treasury yield moved higher from 4.15% to 4.17% 1 .

Portfolio Review

  • The Fund returned 1.27%, reflecting performance at the net asset value (NAV) of Class I shares with all distributions reinvested, for the quarter ended December 31st , 2025. The Fund's benchmark, the ICE BofA 1-3 Year U.S. Corporate Index 5 returned 1.22% during the same period.
  • The portfolio's allocation to short-term high yield corporate bonds was the primary contributor to relative performance, partially given the sector's greater carry. Within high yield corporate bonds, we maintain a focus on higher quality parts of the market trading to a short final maturity or near-term call.
  • Security selection within investment grade corporate bonds also contributed to relative performance, mainly within the Energy, Financial and Communication sectors. Overall, we reduced the portfolio's exposure to investment grade corporates during the quarter, selling shorter-dated bonds trading at tighter valuations while selectively adding to higher-conviction positions during the bouts of market volatility.
  • The portfolio's rates positioning led to a positive impact on relative performance. In particular, the portfolio's modestly longer duration contributed to relative performance as short-term rates moved lower over the quarter.
  • The portfolio's allocation to CMBS was the primary detractor from relative performance as concerns over fundamentals continued to weigh on segments of commercial real estate. Within the sector, we favor AAA-rated Conduit deals with significant credit protection, strong liquidity and diversification benefits.
  • There were no other significant detractors from relative performance.

Outlook

  • We believe the opportunity set for fixed income remains compelling as Treasury yields across the curve are near the highest levels of the past decade. Higher bond yields bring improved return expectations and a more attractive risk/reward dynamic for investors, as the higher starting yields can provide protection should rates rise.
  • After the most recent cut, Fed Fund futures are currently pricing several more cuts over the next year. While it's unclear whether the Fed will be able to fully complete the anticipated program, we believe the bias to ease and prioritization of the labor side of the dual mandate is supportive of credit. Additionally, the economy remains on solid footing, underpinned by relatively strong corporate earnings, resilient consumer fundamentals, and the potential for incremental support from fiscal policy. Although inflation is likely to remain above the Fed's long-term target, we view the risk of a meaningful reacceleration as low. That said, uncertainty remains elevated amid ongoing shifts in geopolitics, trade, immigration, fiscal, and regulatory policies, and we continue to closely monitor for signs of a more pronounced weakening in labor market conditions.
  • We maintain a balanced risk posture, and given the tightness of credit spreads, have positioned the portfolio to generally emphasize carry rather than capital appreciation. Within corporates, we are conservatively positioned, favoring money center banks, energy companies with strong balance sheets, select mega-cap technology firms, diversified insurance and financing businesses along with more defensive segments like Utilities. In the securitized portion of the portfolio, we favor highly liquid, AAA-rated conduit commercial mortgage-backed securities diversified across real estate assets, as well as higher-rated, shorter-average-life collateralized loan obligations and asset backed securities. Given technical pressures, we have found attractive relative value in non-agency mortgage-backed securities with structures consistent with the short duration mandate.
  • Short and intermediate-term bonds have historically delivered attractive absolute performance on average in the one-year period following the first Fed rate cut in previous cycles, while money market and cash equivalent rates have adjusted lower. As we enter the Fed cutting cycle, investors should consider whether cash allocations should be redeployed in short and intermediate-term bonds.

data.title: Cisco Q2 Earnings: The Wrong Way To Get On The AI Bandwagon
data.description:
Cisco Systems, Inc.'s (CSCO) fiscal Q2 2026 earnings release was not well-received by investors, in spite of the strong revenue growth and the expected strong demand in the company's networking segment.

Guidance for the upcoming quarter also did not disappoint as far as revenue growth is concerned, but margins tell a different story. The quarterly dividend was increased, and this sends a strong signal of Cisco's anticipated cash flow stability.

All that was not enough to appease investors, and the stock is down by roughly 10% in the early trading hours.

If we zoom out, over the past year CSCO is still up by 23%, but as we can see on the graph below, this is largely due to broader market movements within the technology space. It was over the past month or so when CSCO's performance broke away from that of the Nasdaq Composite Index and its stock price skyrocketed. This short-term outperformance, however, is now being questioned after the release of the Q2 report.

What's also worth noting is that the very recent jump in CSCO's share price was not accompanied by higher trading volume, which signals a lack of investor conviction.

All in all, CSCO's recent outperformance appears to be largely narrative-driven as the company makes a stronger push into the artificial intelligence (AI) space with its networking chip (more on that later). This puts enormous pressure on Cisco's management to deliver outstanding results - both on growth and margins—and thus even the relatively good second Q2 report came as a disappointment.

Robust Growth

One major thing that the Q2 2026 report achieved was to dispel any fears that Cisco's growth is slowing down to single-digit rates. Total revenue grew at 10% on a year-on-year basis, which was a step up from the 8% growth rate in the prior two 3-month periods.

As expected, the Networking segment is the one doing the heavy lifting with year-on-year growth of 21% during Q2. The rest of Cisco's business units, however, grew at much lower rates, with Security and Services even contracting during the period.

This highlights the importance of Cisco's Silicon One architecture, which is optimized for hyperscalers and data centers and aimed at meeting their requirements for networking solutions. During the Q2 earnings call, Cisco's management stressed the importance of Silicon One and their expectations for growth to remain robust at least until fiscal year 2029.

AI infrastructure orders taken from hyperscalers totaled $2.1 billion in Q2 compared to $1.3 billion just last quarter and equal to the total orders taken in all of fiscal year '25, marking another significant acceleration in growth across our silicon, systems and optics. We shipped our 1 millionth Silicon One chip in Q2 and plan to deploy our Silicon One architecture across our high-performance networking systems by fiscal year '29.

Source: CSCO Q2 2026 Earnings Transcript.

That's all very encouraging, but the issue when thinking of CSCO as a pure growth company is the fact that the Networking segment makes up roughly half of the company's total revenue figure. Thus, even if growth rates in Networking remain at around 20%, the overall company's growth will likely be limited to high single- to low double-digit rates.

Interestingly, sell-side analysts are not fully convinced in such a scenario, with the consensus estimates for CSCO's forward revenue growth rate gravitating around 6%.

The guidance provided for the upcoming 3-month period assumes revenue to be around $15.5bn, which implies that YOY growth would remain at low double digits.

As a result of all that, one could be easily misled that the market is not fully on board with Cisco's anticipated business performance, and this could be interpreted as reason enough for the stock to be undervalued.

The reality, however, is that CSCO stock remains richly priced, and once we factor in expected profitability, then future prospects for high shareholder returns no longer look as appealing.

Not A Value Stock Anymore

Within the context of everything said above, we should start off with the fact that CSCO is no longer priced as a value stock. Just two years ago, CSCO was trading at earnings multiples in the low teens. Right now, even after factoring in the sharp daily decline, CSCO is priced at nearly 29 times earnings, which is a multiple akin to a growth stock.

A higher earnings multiple is to be expected, as Cisco went from a company with declining revenue to one that is now growing at double-digit rates. The problem emerges once we incorporate profitability into the picture.

For quite some time I have been highlighting the importance of gross margins as a key driver of Cisco's valuation multiples. Last January, I downgraded CSCO to a Hold primarily based on the company's capital allocation process with a doubtful acquisition and a heavy debt load. Back then, I didn't have a problem with CSCO's valuation, but this is now becoming an issue.

As we could see on the graph below, over the long term, margins and CSCO's price/sales multiple exhibit a fairly strong relationship, and based on the current gross margin, the stock trades at a significantly elevated multiple.

The graph above should be used only as a reference point and is by no means a definitive sign of whether CSCO is over or undervalued. It does show, however, that the problem with profitability is real and is something that investors care about.

Contrary to the accelerating revenue growth in recent quarters, gross margin has gone in the other direction - falling from as high as 66% in the first quarter of fiscal year 2025 to 65% during Q2 of 2026.

What spooked investors was the guidance for Q3 of this year, with GAAP gross margin expected to be within the range of 63% to 64%. If this comes true, that would be a notable decline in the company's profitability.

Although it is concerning, the declining gross margin is not yet a reason to sell CSCO. As the company grows, economies of scale kick in, and that's one of the main reasons for the gradual increase in the overall return on tangible assets in recent years. The increase, however, is also largely attributed to the $28bn deal for Splunk in 2024, which caused an initial drop in the company's return on tangible assets.

* calculated as adjusted operating income/tangible assets.

With Splunk's acquisition, CSCO has pivoted heavily towards a more recurring revenue-based model. A business model that is now coming under scrutiny as annual changes in annual recurring revenue ((ARR) have gone from 22% in Q2 of 2025 to only 3% as of the last report.

Conclusion

After the release of the fiscal Q2 report, CSCO's investors got a reality check that the company is not a pure growth stock, while it was certainly priced as one. Valuation has now somewhat cooled off, but it is still elevated when considering where margins are headed. In spite of the strong momentum in Networking, the prospects of margin pressures are overwhelming. Moreover, the pivot towards a recurring revenue-based business model has now come to a halt, and that's yet another disappointment for shareholders. Overall, the Q2 report has shown that investors' optimism in recent weeks was premature, and CSCO stock should most likely remain under pressure for the time being.

data.title: APAC Financial Markets Spotlight - Quarterly Report: February 2026
data.description:
Equity momentum extends into 2026

APAC equity strength, especially in Tech, extended gains into 2026. The fundamental outlook for Chinese equities has potential further upside. Australia hiked its policy rate due to higher inflation pressure, leading to AUD outperformance. The BoJ resumed hiking in December, with JGB curve steepening. Nonetheless, JPY weakness persisted.

Equity momentum remained strong across APAC through 2025 and continued into early 2026. Tech-led markets, including Korea, Taiwan, and Japan, extended their rallies. While valuations have become more expensive, earnings forecasts have also been revised higher, offering fundamental support. Singapore financials continued to demonstrate resilience. Notably, China’s equity market outperformed the US in 2025, the first time since 2020. The Chinese market has become more balanced in sector exposure relative to 2015, whereas the US has skewed further toward Technology.

data.description:
Investment Strategy

Primarily invests in a focused portfolio of approximately 40 holdings of mid cap companies identified through our fundamental research as demonstrating promising growth potential. Seeks long-term capital appreciation.

Benchmark

Highlights

  • During the fourth quarter of 2025, the largest portfolio sector weightings were Information Technology and Industrials. The largest sector overweight was Information Technology and the largest sector underweight was Consumer Discretionary.
  • The Information Technology and Utilities sectors contributed to relative performance while Health Care and Consumer Discretionary were among sectors that detracted from relative performance.

Market Environment

U.S. equities ended the fourth quarter on a firm footing, with the S&P 500 Index returning 2.7%, extending the steady grind upward that characterized 2025, after the tariff announcements. Investor confidence was supported by a familiar trio: corporate earnings that came in better than expected, a Federal Reserve (Fed) that pushed further into an easing stance, and a macroeconomic backdrop that — despite mounting cross-currents — remained resilient in the aggregate. Market sentiment also benefited from improving clarity on trade policy, including an extension of the U.S. – China truce and selective tariff relief aimed at easing consumer affordability pressures.

At the same time, the quarter was defined by widening dispersion beneath the index-level surface. The market's biggest tailwind — continued investment tied to artificial intelligence (AI) — was increasingly accompanied by scrutiny around AI infrastructure bottlenecks, sources of financing, and whether the AI theme could deliver returns commensurate with the capital deployed. Macroeconomic risks also remained in play: labor market softening became harder to ignore, consumers appeared more cautious and bifurcated across income cohorts, and housing stayed under pressure.

Complicating the read-through, a prolonged government shutdown reduced economic data visibility, amplifying debate over inflation and employment signals just as global central-bank dynamics leaned less uniformly supportive. Nevertheless, U.S. equities reinforced a constructive baseline for the S&P 500 Index, where Health Care was the top performing sector, while Real Estate was the weakest.

During the quarter, we continued to observe secular themes that we believe are creating attractive investment opportunities—corporations are digitizing their operations, cloud computing is growing and supporting innovation, and AI is at an inflection point, potentially enabling significant increases in productivity. Moreover, U.S. business spending appears to be accelerating, driven by a confluence of factors—most notably burgeoning demand for AI infrastructure and tax incentives from the One Big Beautiful Bill, which allow companies to deduct 100% of eligible equipment and qualifying manufacturing-facility costs in the year placed in service, boosting near-term cash flow and after-tax returns.

Portfolio Update

The Alger Mid Cap 40 ETF underperformed the Russell Midcap Growth Index during the fourth quarter of 2025. Exact Sciences Corporation (EXAS), Micron Technology, Inc. (MU) and MongoDB, Inc. (MDB) were among the top contributors to performance.

  • Exact Sciences is a molecular diagnostics company focused on oncology testing, best known for its Cologuard noninvasive colorectal cancer screening franchise, alongside a broader portfolio and pipeline that includes molecular residual disease (MRD) and multi-cancer early detection (MCED) testing initiatives. Shares contributed positively during the quarter after Abbott announced a definitive all-cash agreement to acquire Exact Sciences for approximately $21 billion in equity value, reflecting a meaningful premium and driving the stock higher.
  • Micron designs and manufactures memory and memory modules, and is one of the world's three major suppliers of DRAM, NAND, and high-bandwidth memory (HBM) serving end markets that include PCs, smartphones, traditional servers, and AI servers. As AI infrastructure has expanded, memory demand has accelerated meaningfully; combined with constrained industry supply, this has driven a sharp rise in memory pricing. Shares contributed positively during the quarter after the company reported better-than-expected fiscal first-quarter operating results. Micron's revenue increased 57% year-over-year and gross margins expanded to above 56%. Management also indicated that HBM capacity is already fully committed through 2026 and that conventional DRAM pricing is expected to remain on an upward trajectory into 2026.
  • MongoDB is a software company that provides a document-oriented database platform, enabling developers to store and manage data in flexible structures compared with traditional SQL databases. The platform is designed for agility and scalability, supporting rapid application development and a wide range of data types and workloads. Shares contributed positively after the company reported strong fiscal third-quarter earnings, including faster revenue growth, rising customer adoption for AI-related workloads, and continued progress on profitability. Management highlighted that Atlas, the company's flagship cloud database service, is growing roughly 30% year over year.

Roblox Corp. (RBLX), Nebius Group (NBIS), and Robinhood Markets, Inc. (HOOD) were among the top detractors from performance.

  • Roblox is a digital platform where users play, learn, communicate, and expand relationships in millions of 3D digital experiences within an open-world metaverse environment. The platform is powered by user-generated content (UGC) with experiences and most items being built by third-party developers and creators, on top of Roblox's development engine. Roblox uses AI to improve recommendations, moderate user-generated content, and help creators build new experiences more efficiently. The company reports bookings when users purchase its virtual currency, Robux, through one-time purchases, prepaid cards, or subscriptions. Shares detracted during the quarter following management's decision to use the surge in engagement and monetization to reinvest into the business through higher capital expenditures and operating expenses. The company plans to allocate capital into building and training their own AI models along with incorporating additional safeguards to weed out inappropriate activities.
  • Nebius is a provider of AI-focused cloud infrastructure, operating GPU compute capacity across Europe and expanding in the U.S. We view the company as a differentiated "neocloud" beneficiary of accelerating AI adoption, as demand for high-quality GPU compute continues to outstrip available supply of chips, power, and data center capacity. Nebius already operates a first-party data center in Finland and has deployed GPU capacity in Paris, while adding U.S. capacity through a Kansas City, Missouri deployment and a new data center build-out in New Jersey. During the quarter, shares detracted amid a broader selloff in AI infrastructure-related equities as investors grew more concerned about the "circularity" of AI ecosystem financing and the durability of AI infrastructure spending. Despite near-term volatility, Nebius continues to sign large, long-term agreements with tier-one customers, including multi-year AI infrastructure contracts with major cloud service providers, improving revenue visibility as new capacity comes online.
  • Robinhood is a consumer-focused financial platform offering commission-free trading in stocks, options, futures and crypto, alongside retirement investing and subscription benefits via Robinhood Gold. The company has recently broadened into wealth management with Robinhood Strategies (expert-managed portfolios), and announced Robinhood Banking and Robinhood Cortex as part of a wider push to deliver advisory, banking and AI-driven tools within the app. The company's revenue mix comes from payment for order flow and commissions, cryptocurrency, equities revenues, futures, net interest income from margin lending and interest earned on customer cash balances, securities lending, and credit cards. During the quarter, shares detracted from performance, primarily due to a sharp decline in cryptocurrency markets.

data.title: Rubrik: Finally In The Value Bin (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
Rubrik, Inc. (RBRK) has slumped more than 50% since the stock hit a wall last year. The enterprise cybersecurity stock has been hit with the AI software sell-off, but the sector probably benefits more from AI growth than not. My investment thesis is now ultra Bullish on Rubrik with the stock looking to bounce off $50.

Built For An AI World

Rubrik builds data identity and resilience products that protect enterprises from cyberattacks where the major risk is the criminal logging into the system. The business is built for an AI world with AI agent adoption and the special identity requirements.

The company is a cyber resilience leader pulling together the data backup and cybersecurity areas to protect customer data in an AI world where enterprise data is ever more valuable. The strong subscription growth would support AI creating more demand to monitor AI agents and backup data, though AI innovations are constantly changing the market dynamics and the future is unknown.

Rubrik Agent Cloud helps enterprises manage risk by monitoring and auditing AI agentic actions, enforcing real-time guardrails for agentic changes, and works to fine-tune agents for accuracy and fix mistakes. In essence, the company appears positioned to benefit from an AI world.

Rubrik is a subscription ARR growth machine with 34% growth in FQ3'26 to reach $1.35 billion with a record $94 million in net new ARR. The company has shown no signs of slowing and guided to ending FY26 with ARR of $1.44 billion for 32% growth

The company forecasting over 30% ARR growth doesn't support AI impacting the business one bit. Rubrik has a history of conservative guidance with the original forecast for the year established at a high of only $1.36 billion.

Value Bin Now

The stock only trades at 7x FY27 revenue targets of $1.6 billion. Naturally, the stock had double the multiple previously when trading above $100, making Rubrik very appealing at this valuation now.

Rubrik is even far cheaper, valuing the stock based on the higher ARR levels. Based on the FY27 revenue target and 30% growth, the stock trades closer to 6x ARR targets in the $1.87 billion range.

The company isn't profitable now with FQ4 guidance for a loss of at least $0.10. The market likes to flip back and forth on whether the corporate profitability matters, so investors are best off not getting too aggressive or pessimistic on both extremes.

Due to the nature of subscriptions, Rubrik was actually cash flow positive in FQ3 with $86 million generated from operations and free cash flow of $77 million. The cyber resilience company has boosted YTD operating cash flows by $225 million YoY, so the issue with Rubrik not being profitable should be overridden by the subscription business starting to throw off strong cash flows.

The company has a massive cash balance of $1.6 billion, though it is offset somewhat with convertible debt of $1.1 billion. The key here is that free cash flow going forward will provide Rubrik with a robust balance sheet to invest in the business or repurchase shares on any further dip.

Rubrik has seen the market cap dip down to just $11 billion. The stock is one of the best values in the cybersecurity space now with some of the highest growth rates and the big dip offers an opportunity not present in a while.

As compared to cybersecurity leaders like CrowdStrike (CRWD) and Palo Alto Networks (PANW), Rubrik trades at around half the forward P/S multiple at only 5.5x. The company is the only company trading at 30% growth while the 2 cybersecurity giants are only growing in the 15% to 20% ranges.

Not Without Risks

The biggest risk is AI spinning up a better cyber resilience product, causing Rubrik to lose business. The AI threat doesn't appear immediate, but AI is advancing very quickly, and enterprise AI software appears the most at risk for replacement.

Other than that, the stock still trades at 7x revenues, and lower valuations are always possible. Rubrik is in the value bin already, but the multiple the market is willing to pay can always get lower.

Takeaway

The key investor takeaway is that Rubrik is finally a value play after trading at rich premiums for a while. Investors should use the current weakness to load up on the cyber resilience stock where AI should only increase demand. The stock has made a solid bounce of $50, providing strong support similar to back in 2025.

data.title: Royalty Pharma: Strong Execution And Visible Pipeline Support Our Buy View
data.description:
Following the Q4 and full-year 2025 results, we revisit Royalty Pharma plc (NASDAQ:RPRX) with a constructive stance. Execution remains solid, and we continue to view the stock as an attractive entry point, supported by resilient figures, recent value-accretive transactions, and a clearer commitment to shareholder returns. Again, we are up by almost 15% since our last update (Q3 results comment - Fig. 1) and >77% since our initiation of coverage (The Time Has Come).

Fig 1

Royalty Pharma Results and Our Positive Take

Royalty Pharma had an exceptionally strong 2025, capped by a stellar fourth quarter. The company is firing on all cylinders: top-line receipts are growing robustly, they are deploying massive amounts of capital into new, high-potential royalties, and they are aggressively returning capital to shareholders.

In numbers, receipts accelerated by 17% to $856 million in Q4. This was supported by Trelegy, Voranigo, and the cystic fibrosis franchise (Fig. 2) . RP's portfolio receipts hit $3.254 billion in 2025, achieving the absolute top end of the guidance they had already raised in Q3. This is a hallmark of a well-managed company.

Regarding capital allocation priorities (Fig. 3), the company deployed $2.6 billion, adding 9 new royalties to the portfolio. They sustained a DPS and repurchased 37 million shares for a total consideration of $1.2 billion in 2025. This was achieved by maintaining a good balance sheet.

Source: RP Q4 results ppt - Fig 2

Fig 3

Why are we positive?

Here at the Lab, we believe consistent execution and solid operating results alone support a Buy stance, even though RP's share price has yet to reach our target level; additional factors underpin our investment case.

Moderate growth, but a de-risked strategy

While the growth rate is expected to moderate in 2026 due to known patent expiries, the foundation is solid, and RP's pipeline is full of catalysts. The company holds royalty interests in more than 10 late-stage assets, several of which are expected to deliver clinical milestones or commercial launches over the next 1 to 2 years. These programs could translate into over $1 billion of peak royalty income. As execution risk declines, they should progressively support revenue visibility, potentially representing close to 17% of the projected 2033 topline. Therefore, we see scope for upward revisions to estimates as these catalysts mature and cash flows become clearer.

Ongoing Capital Deployment

In parallel, the company has continued to deploy capital actively, committing more than $3 billion per year to new royalty opportunities since 2022. Key to report is the fact that 67% of the $2.6 billion capital deployment in 2025 were commercially available drugs. This supports resilient earnings. Given its strong underwriting track record, we have raised our assumptions for future deployment and asset success rates. We reported in the initial paragraph a new royalty acquisition for RP. Indeed, on December-end and already in 2026, the company announced with Teva and Evrysdi. The two drugs could have blockbuster potential. In detail, on the Teva deal, the company sees Vitiligo as a large market, with IL-15 as a fundamental to the biology and physiology of the disease.

Right Capital Allocation Priorities and Upside Ahead

Here at the Lab, it is not the first time we have reported supportive Capital Allocation Priorities. So, we report the recent increase in DPS. This reinforces management’s confidence in cash generation and further supports the equity story. Additionally, at the current valuation and given the pipeline ahead, we continue to see an attractive risk/reward on the equity story. We should also report the latest biotech market outperformance. This is likely to attract more generalists to the sector, and the RP share price will benefit from increased investment inflows.

Valuation

Despite losses of drug exclusivity, the company anticipates growth in 2026. RP is well known to provide beat and raise. Aside from the RP's pipeline upside catalyst, of the $2.6 billion deployed in 2025, 67% was for already approved, commercially available products. This is a key part of RP's strategy to balance risk. The company has no change in its annual EPS growth rate of approximately 12% until 2030. Still, for production reasons, given the lower growth expected, we imply a portfolio receipts growth rate of 8%. This is at the upper end of the company's guidance (Fig. 4) and represents lower growth than our previous estimate of 9%. RP has a lean structure and well represents its interest rate payment and costs. This is supported by RP Management incorporation. So, with $3.4 billion in top-line sales, $204 million in operating costs (6% of sales), and $350 million in interest payments, we reached a net profit of €2.85 billion. Even without including potential buybacks, with the current 577.24 million shares outstanding, our EPS is$4.93. Continuing to apply a 10x P/E target, our value moved from $48.5 to $49.3 per share.

Fig 4

Risks

We have a long coverage of RP downside risks. The major risk is the Vertex franchise development. Additionally, we report a lower portfolio receipts growth in 2026. This is due to loss of exclusivity. RP is also exposed to increased competition and the entry of other players into the market. Given its debt, RP is sensitive to changes in interest rates. Businesswise (Fig. 5), key FDA approvals include Myqorzo (formerly aficamten) and Johnson & Johnson's Tremfya in new indications. We also report TEV-'749 (schizophrenia), Trodelvy (breast cancer), and deucrictibant (hereditary angioedema), setting them up for future approvals and royalty streams.

Fig 5

Conclusion

We believe Royalty Pharma is in a very strong position. 2025 was a year of outstanding execution, growth, and strategic investment. The company is proactively managing its portfolio and has a clear growth plan. The outlook for 2026 is solid and realistic, reflecting both the portfolio's strengths and its challenges. We continue to see upside ahead, given a valuation not fully priced in. So, we remain buyers.
